question: As a journalist, Bala has always had a <u>nose</u> for stories
section: In each of questions 56 to 65, choose the option nearest in meaning to the word or phrase underlined.
solution:
In this context, to have a nose is to have the ability to sense or find something, such as news, trouble, gossip, etc.

Example; She really has a nose for news. She's a good reporter.

section: In each of the questions, the word in capital letters has the emphatic stress, Choose the option to which the given sentence relates.
solution: In answering questions on emphatic stress,  the answer in direct reversal to the one in block form and that seeks to clearly restate and provoke the statement is the most suitable. This is why did the cashier pay the workers salary in september is right.

question: The man reasoned that there ought to be a limit to sycophancy

option.a: there can be no favour beyond a reasonable point
option.b: sycophants need not talk all the time
option.c: people should know when not to use flattery
option.d: sycophants should know when to grant people requests

section: Choose the option that best explains the information conveyed in the sentence.
solution:
sycophancy;  using of flattery compliments to gain self-serving favour or advantage from another person.

Option C is correct. 
''The man reasoned that there ought to be a limit to sycophancy'' means that, they should be a limit to how people use flattery compliments on people especially when they stand to gain something in return.

question: choose the option opposite in meaning to the word or phrase in italics They come here on bootless errand
solution:
The word bootless means ineffectual, useless of a task or undertaking, while the answer worthwhile means worth the time, effort spent
Bootless explains that the errand was useless while worthwhile explains it was the effort, which is also synonymous to the word useful
